Table of contents

    Click anywhere outside this panel, or press Esc to close.

    Agentic Coding
    for Beginners

    A 90-minute hands-on introduction.

    Stefan Geißler

    What this is, and what it isn't

    ✓ This is

    A snapshot of how agentic coding tools work as of June 2026: a brief background and a hands-on tour of opencode + Matt Pocock's skill collection. The goal is to provide enough information so you can decide for yourself what's worth your time.

    ✗ This is not

    A vibe-coding tutorial.
    A "use ChatGPT for everything" pitch.
    A survey of every agentic tool on the market.
    A model-finetuning lecture.

    Agenda

    • LLM basics: tokens, context windows, and why models only see text
    • Harness anatomy: prompts, tool schemas, files, history, and feedback loops
    • Agentic concepts: skills, agents, subagents, and when each helps
    • Tool landscape: why opencode, and how its terminal workflow feels
    • Setup path: install opencode, add skills, and inspect the TUI
    • Use cases: applying skills to code, text, and slides

    The 90-minute plan

    Theory first — about 30 minutes. Then hands-on for the rest.

    00:00 → 00:30 · theory

    Background

    LLM basics, the model vs harness distinction, context, skills / agents / subagents, why the harness matters, why opencode, why Matt Pocock's skills.

    00:30 → 01:30 · hands-on

    Workshop

    Install opencode, chat with the sample app, install Matt Pocock's skills, then work through three hands-on use cases: code generation, a measurement-study outline, and a deck built with deck.js — each progressing from plain prompts to the full skill stack (/grill-me, /tdd, /to-prd, /to-issues).

    The LLM and the agent

    Two terms, two things. The LLM is the driver. The agent is what you get when you wrap an LLM in a harness.

    LLM

    The model itself

    A next-token predictor. Nothing more. The same LLM powers ChatGPT, Claude Code, opencode, and your curl call to the API. The "brain in a box".

    agent

    LLM  +  harness

    An LLM, plus the loop that feeds it context, the tools it can call, the memory it accumulates, and the UI you type into. The thing you actually interact with.

    The LLM sits inside the agent. The harness is everything wrapped around it.

    The next 30 minutes unpack one of those two things at a time — first the LLM, then the harness, then the loop that ties them together. By the end, you should be able to point at any agentic tool and say: "that's the LLM, and that's the harness".

    What an LLM actually does

    One job. Repeated billions of times.

    iteration 1 — waiting

    Given all the text so far, the model assigns a probability to every possible next token and samples one. The predicted token is appended to the context, and the loop continues — one token at a time.

    That's it. No retrieval, no reasoning engine, no planning module, no memory beyond the window. Everything else you see in an "agent" is added around the model.

    Tokens — the unit the model sees

    Models don't read characters or words. They read tokens: chunks of text drawn from a fixed vocabulary of roughly 50–100k entries. Common words survive as one token; rarer words get split into sub-word pieces.

    the whole word  ·  ization sub-word piece
    Click any token to inspect it.
    Pick a token above to inspect.
    tokens: 0
    characters: 0
    model cost: roughly $0.000000 per call (at GPT-class rates)

    Why this matters: context window size is measured in tokens, not characters or words. A 200k-token context is roughly 150k English words. Every system prompt, every tool result, every file you @-mention — it all eats the same budget.

    The context window

    The model's working memory. It is finite, it is shared, and it is the single most important constraint to design around.

    0 / 200,000 tokens
    raw: 0 compacted: 0
    ⚡ Compaction triggered — context exceeded the window. The harness summarized the oldest items (at ~20% of original size). Detail is lost; the model can still see what was there, just not verbatim.

    Click things to add them to the context:

    When the window fills up, the harness doesn't panic — it compacts: the oldest content gets summarized down to a fraction of its original size. Modern harnesses manage this for you, but the principle holds: everything the model knows in this turn is what's in the window right now.

    The model is the brain. The harness is the hands.

    This is the single most important distinction in this whole lecture.

    The model is the same model whether you talk to it via ChatGPT, Claude Code, opencode, or a curl call to the API. The harness is everything else: the tool loop, the file access, the memory, the command palette, the user interface.

    Swap the harness, keep the model — and the capability changes dramatically. Swap the model, keep the harness — and the output style changes, but the structural capability stays roughly the same.

    Same model, different harness, different agent.

    Most people use a chat UI. The harness turns the same model into something else.

    chat UI

    One-shot Q&A

    You paste a snippet, it replies with text. No files, no execution, no memory between sessions. Most people's mental model of "AI".

    IDE extension

    Inline assist

    Tab-completion on the current line, or a side panel that sees the open files. Powerful for typing, weak for multi-step work.

    agent harness

    Loop + tools

    Read files, run shell, edit, test, iterate. Long-horizon. This is the mode we're talking about today — and the one that compounds.

    at
    CONTEXT
    user: "fix the failing test in @tests/test_api.py"
→ prompt lands at the start of the context window
    iteration 1 first turn

    An agent harness is a loop: the model produces output, the harness executes the tool calls in that output, feeds the results back, and asks for the next move. This loop is the entire magic. Everything else is polish.

    What goes into the context window

    The model knows only what is currently in the window. You decide what goes in.

    system System prompt — the model's role, constraints, tone ~1–4k tokens
    skills Loaded skill instructions (we'll come back to this) ~0.5–2k tokens each
    history Past messages, tool calls, tool results grows over time
    tools Tool schemas — what the model is allowed to call ~1–5k tokens
    @files Files you've @-mentioned or that the agent has read whatever they need
    user Your most recent message ~tens–hundreds of tokens

    An agent harness composes all of these for you, on every turn. Skills, files, history, tool schemas — they're not magic. They're literally text being prepended to your prompt.

    System prompt — the model's job description

    A static preamble, set by the harness or by an agent config. The first thing the model sees on every turn, and the only thing it sees verbatim. Everything else is user-supplied or tool-returned.

    A typical system prompt looks like this — truncated, but recognisable:

    # role
You are an expert software engineer working in a checked-out git repo.
You prefer small, focused changes. You write tests before fixing bugs.

# constraints
- Never run destructive commands (rm -rf, force-push, drop table) without asking.
- Never edit files outside the working directory.
- Never invent file paths. If you have not read a file, say so.

# output format
Reply in markdown. Use fenced code blocks for code. Cite file paths as
`path/to/file.ts:42` so the user can jump.

# tools
Use the bash, read, write, and edit tools. Prefer edit over write
for existing files. One tool call per turn unless you genuinely need parallel.
    role

    Who the model is

    "You are a senior staff engineer." Sets defaults: what it pays attention to, what it skips, how cautious it is.

    rules

    What it must / must not do

    Hard constraints. Most of the time, the system prompt is mostly rules. This is where you put "ask before running migrations".

    format

    How it should reply

    Markdown, code-block conventions, citation style, length limits. Cheap to set, surprisingly large effect on output quality.

    Whoever writes the system prompt writes the agent's personality. The harness ships a default; an agent definition in opencode.json can override it. Same model, different prompt, different agent.

    Tool schemas — the menu, written in JSON

    Every tool the model can use is described in text. The description is sent on every turn. The model "calls" a tool by emitting JSON that matches the schema — the harness parses it and runs the real thing.

    A tool definition in JSON Schema:

    {
  "name": "read",
  "description": "Read the contents of a file at the given path.",
  "parameters": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "filePath": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "Absolute path to the file to read."
      },
      "offset": { "type": "number", "description": "Line to start from." },
      "limit":  { "type": "number", "description": "Max lines to return." }
    },
    "required": ["filePath"]
  }
}
    cost

    You pay every turn

    Every tool description is re-sent on every turn. 20 tools × 200 tokens ≈ 4k tokens of overhead before the user says a word. This is why "more tools" is not a feature.

    visibility

    The model picks from what's in the prompt

    If a tool isn't in the schema, the model cannot call it. Disable a tool, and that whole class of action is gone. No prompt injection can re-enable it.

    hallucination

    Models invent tool names

    Without a schema, the model will confidently call run_tests or fix_bug that don't exist. The schema is what grounds it in the real interface.

    Good tool descriptions read like a tiny API doc. A vague "do something useful" description is a vague tool. The harness re-pays your writing effort on every single turn.

    Skills — packaged instructions, loaded on demand

    A skill is a small bundle: instructions for the model, plus sometimes scripts. The agent loads it when you invoke the name.

    What's in a skill

    • A short name — the command you type to invoke it.
    • Instructions — markdown the model reads when the skill loads. The whole point of the skill.
    • Optional scripts — helpers the model can call (rare; most skills are just text).

    opencode looks for skills in two places:

    • ~/.agents/skills/ — user-level. This is where the Matt Pocock skills land after the installer runs.
    • .opencode/skills/ — project-level, committed to the repo so the team shares the same skills.

    Skills are how you teach the agent your preferences once and never explain them again.

    Agents — persistent, named configurations

    A skill is a tip. An agent is a personality.

    An agent is a named slot with its own system prompt, its own set of allowed tools, and its own model. opencode lets you define custom agents for specialist roles:

    Each agent is a markdown file under .config/opencode/agents/ with YAML frontmatter for permissions and model settings. You can Tab between them in the opencode TUI.

    Subagents — agents that spawn other agents

    When the work decomposes, the parent agent can delegate.

    Click any agent to see its full instructions. The parent delegates and merges results.

    Why bother: context isolation. Each subagent has its own clean window, so a long task doesn't pollute the parent's history with thousands of tokens of intermediate work. The parent only sees the final reports.

    The harness landscape

    You are not short on choices. They mostly differ on host environment and model lock-in.

    HarnessHostModelLicense
    Claude Code terminal Anthropic only proprietary
    Codex CLI terminal OpenAI open source
    Aider terminal any open source
    Cursor / Windsurf / Copilot IDE various proprietary
    pi terminal any open source
    opencode terminal + IDE + web 75+ providers open source

    Why opencode for this lecture

    • Open source. You can read every line, you can fork it, you can use a pinned version in a research work.
    • Model-agnostic. opencode talks to basically every LLM provider out there. The harness is the asset, not the model. Today we are going to use the free models included with opencode.
    • Terminal-native. opencode is designed as a TUI first; it works over SSH; it doesn't need a GUI; GUI and web versions are available.
    • Real LSP integration. opencode comes with language server support out of the box.
    • First-class skill support. Reads SKILL.md files, both user-level (~/.config/opencode/skills/) and project-level (.opencode/skills/).

    Step 1 — install opencode (5 min)

    You probably have it already. If not, this is all you need.

    1

    Run the install script

    One-liner, downloads a static binary. Works on macOS, Linux, and WSL.

    # macOS / Linux / WSL
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash
    2

    Or: install via your package manager

    npm install -g opencode-ai          # Node.js
brew install anomalyco/tap/opencode # macOS / Linux
pacman -S opencode                  # Arch
    3

    Configure a model provider

    opencode needs an API key for some model. Easiest path: /connect in the TUI and follow the prompts. Or set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / OPENAI_API_KEY in your shell.

    4

    Verify

    $ opencode --version
1.x.x

    Step 2 — install Matt Pocock's skills (5 min)

    One command. The skills land in ~/.config/opencode/skills/.

    1

    Run the universal installer

    Works for opencode, Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Codex, and 15+ other agents — same syntax, different destination.

    $ npx skills@latest add mattpocock/skills

    You'll be prompted to (1) pick which skills to install, and (2) which agents to install them on. Select opencode. You don't have to take all of them — for this lecture, the ones you actually need are grill-me, tdd, to-prd, to-issues, and setup-matt-pocock-skills.

    2

    What just happened

    opencode now has access to those skills as commands. Type / in the TUI to see them — they'll show up as /grill-me, /tdd, etc.

    Step 3 — the one-time setup (3 min)

    Some of the engineering skills need to know where you keep things. Run this once per repo.

    1

    Run /setup-matt-pocock-skills inside opencode

    It will quiz you — three or four short questions:

    • Which issue tracker do you use? (GitHub Issues / Linear / local markdown / none)
    • Which labels do you apply when triaging? (e.g. bug, enhancement, chore)
    • Where do you want domain docs saved? (e.g. docs/)

    For the lecture, pick local markdown for the tracker (we're not wiring up GitHub), and accept the default labels. The setup writes a small AGENTS.md section so the other skills know where to put things.

    Matt Pocock's skills — the four failure modes

    The collection is structured around the four ways agents fail. Each skill is the fix.

    #1
    The agent didn't do what I want

    You think you told it. It thinks you told it something else. The misalignment problem.

    Fix → /grill-me · /grill-with-docs (interview you until the spec is sharp)
    #2
    The agent is way too verbose

    It uses 20 words where 1 will do. No shared vocabulary, no shared domain language.

    Fix → CONTEXT.md (a shared-language file the agent reads on every turn)
    #3
    The code doesn't work

    No feedback loop, so the agent is flying blind. It can't tell working from broken.

    Fix → /tdd (red-green-refactor) · /diagnose (for hard bugs)
    #4
    We built a ball of mud

    Agents accelerate code production — and they accelerate entropy just as fast.

    Fix → /zoom-out · /to-prd · /to-issues (deliberate architecture investment)

    Skills — the collection

    A skill is a small bundle: instructions for the model, plus sometimes scripts. The agent loads it when you invoke the name. Click each card to see what it does.

    Skills are how you teach the agent your preferences once and never explain them again. The mattpocock/skills collection is one curated set — a great default, forkable, and you can write your own.

    A tour of the opencode TUI (1 min)

    A working session: the previous turn in the log, a fresh prompt waiting at the bottom. Four regions to know before you start typing.

    1. Conversation log

      The big panel on the left. One block per turn; the header is agent · model · wall-clock duration. Inside, + Thought: marks the model's reasoning, ⊕ marks a tool call, and a final block summarises the result. Scroll up to revisit earlier turns.

    2. Sidebar — context & LSP

      The narrow column on the right. Context is the live token budget for this session (141k / 200k → 28% used, $0.20 spent). LSP lights up language servers as files are read — that's the harness wiring real code intelligence into the model.

    3. Prompt input

      The single-line box at the bottom of the log — where you type. Prefix with / to invoke a skill (/grill-me, /tdd, /setup-matt-pocock-skills). The line below shows the active agent, the model, and a live thinking indicator while a turn is running.

    4. Status bar

      Token count, $ spent, ctrl+p for the command palette, and the opencode version. Everything you need to monitor cost or jump modes is one keystroke away.

    From “write me a function” to test-driven agents

    The same task, four times. Each step adds a skill and removes one failure mode. Click an exercise for the detail.

    Common pitfalls
    • Copy-paste the first answer — no /grill-me step, misalignment hidden.
    • Skip the red step — no feedback loop, code looks right.
    • One mega-PR — no /to-issues slicing, unreviewable diff.

    From “outline my paper” to a measurement study with a shared vocabulary

    Scientific writing is the failure mode that hurts most — agents are fluent liars. We outline a measurement study on sustainability in data center networks, four times. Each step adds a skill and removes one failure mode. Click an exercise for the detail.

    Common pitfalls
    • Agents invent datasets and testbeds — no /grill-me grounding in what you can actually measure.
    • Agents conflate metrics — no glossary to keep “energy efficiency” and “power consumption” distinct.
    • Agents smooth over methodology gaps — no ADR for what was not measured and why.

    From “make me 10 slides” to a deck with a shared design language

    Building slides with an agent is meta — deck.js built this very deck. The same four-step progression, with one extra: learn the design language first. Click an exercise for the detail.

    Common pitfalls
    • Skip DESIGN.md — off-brand decks, every agent invents a new look.
    • Skip the grill — doc-dump decks with no claims, no narrative.
    • Skip the slice — one mega-HTML file, hand-edited, breaks the build.

    This deck — the one you’re looking at — was built with the discipline on this slide. The first revision was one HTML file. The current revision is 26 folders, each a vertical slice.

    What to try next

    You've used five skills. There are twenty more in the collection. Here are four worth pulling on next.

    1
    Take a real bug to /diagnose

    Find something broken in a project you actually care about. Run the diagnose skill. Notice the red-green discipline it imposes — the model is forbidden from guessing before reproducing.

    2
    Run /zoom-out on an unfamiliar file

    Open a project you didn't write. Pick a file that scares you. Ask the agent to explain it in the context of the whole system. Compare to reading the file cold — the framing is the value.

    3
    Write a CONTEXT.md for a project you own

    Pick a real codebase. Spend 30 minutes distilling its jargon into a one-page shared language. Watch the next session of opencode against that project become twice as productive.

    4
    Wire opencode against a local model

    Install Ollama, pull a small model, point opencode at it via opencode.json. Notice the capability drop — and notice that for narrow tasks, the drop is smaller than you expected.

    Resources

    Everything in this deck, cited and linked.

    OpenCode ↗

    The harness. 75+ model providers, terminal-native, open source, LSP-aware.

    mattpocock/skills ↗

    The skill collection. Twenty-plus skills covering the four common failure modes. MIT-licensed, forkable, designed to be hacked on.

    skills.sh ↗

    The skills directory. Where you find what other people have packaged. Browseable, ranked by install count.

    AI Hero newsletter ↗

    Matt Pocock's newsletter. The cleanest signal in the agentic-coding space, in my experience.

    OpenCode Discord ↗

    Where to ask opencode-specific questions and talk to the maintainers.

    Anthropic — Building effective agents ↗

    The original "workflows vs agents" framing. Worth the 20-minute read.

    End of deck. Press Esc for the table of contents, or ← to go back.
